title: Housing isn’t just a young people problem. We know how this story ends
summary: A report from the Actuaries Institute highlights that while younger Australians have seen improvements in health and education, they face significant economic and housing challenges compared to older generations. The wealth gap is widening, driven by government spending favouring older Australians and persistent housing affordability issues. This situation may lead to increased intergenerational inequity, with younger people bearing the financial burden of supporting older generations.
publication:
Housing isn’t just a young people problem. We know how this story ends
theage.com.au30 April 2026 19:00 GMT1,415 wordsMillie Muroi

When we debate intergenerational fairness, it often looks like younger Australians lamenting the selfishness of Boomers and those Boomers calling Zoomers “entitled” for wanting more.
key_points: Younger Australians have gained in health and education but face economic challenges.
Government spending is increasingly directed towards older generations, worsening intergenerational fairness.
Housing affordability remains a critical issue, impacting young people's ability to build wealth.
relevance: Understanding these trends is crucial for actuaries and financial services professionals as they assess risks and develop strategies for wealth management across generations.

title: Insurers throw weight behind national plan to slash premiums for resilient homes
summary: Australia's insurance industry is supporting a national plan to reduce home insurance premiums by incentivising homeowners to upgrade their properties for disaster resilience. The Housing Resilience Action Plan 2030 aims to link lower insurance costs to resilience improvements, addressing the rising affordability stress affecting 1.6 million households. Stakeholders, including IAG and the Insurance Council of Australia, emphasise the need for coordinated action across government, industry, and communities to tackle the underlying risks and improve long-term affordability.

title: Insurers among group proposing cheaper premiums in exchange for more resilient homes
summary: A coalition of insurers, actuaries, banks, and community advocates has proposed a plan to reduce home insurance premiums by linking them to resilience upgrades for homes against natural disasters. The initiative aims to address the rising insurance affordability stress affecting 1.6 million Australian households, driven by increasing natural disasters and building costs. The proposed National Risk and Resilience Rating System (NRRRS) seeks to enhance home resilience while providing clearer information to consumers about their insurance options.

title: Set-up balance not critical for AFCA
summary: The Australian Financial Complaints Authority (AFCA) has clarified that the minimum balance for establishing a Self-Managed Super Fund (SMSF) is not the sole factor in assessing complaints. Cost-effectiveness must also be evaluated in relation to the client's current superannuation vehicle and anticipated contributions.

title: APRA amends CPS 230 for non‑traditional service providers
summary: APRA has amended Prudential Standard CPS 230 to introduce a limited exemption for certain non-traditional service providers, effective July 1, 2026. However, all other CPS 230 requirements remain in effect, emphasising the need for ongoing operational risk management.

title: Australia lacks national strategy to regulate AI spread in workplace, report states

It highlights the need for clear regulations to protect workers' rights and ensure responsible AI deployment.

title: Property on shaky ground as rate hikes loom in 2026
summary: Australia's household wealth reached a record high of $18.85 trillion, primarily driven by property values, which rose to $12.53 trillion. However, with rising interest rates expected in 2026, there are concerns about potential declines in property prices and the need for households to reassess their property investments. Financial experts suggest increasing exposure to shares as a more accessible and less interest-sensitive investment alternative.

title: 2 billion reasons to fix retirement income
summary: A new proposal highlights the need to reform Australia's retirement income system, which currently allows over 1.5 million Australians aged 65 and over to hold $326 billion in 'stranded balances' that incur unnecessary tax. The proposed 'MyIncome' initiative would facilitate the transition of superannuation funds into pension phase, ensuring members begin drawing income by age 75. This change aims to enhance retirement wealth and align the superannuation system with its intended purpose of providing income in retirement.

title: Labor’s NDIS cuts leave many questions unanswered. Here’s what we know so far
summary: The Australian government has announced significant changes to the National Disability Insurance Scheme (NDIS), shifting eligibility from diagnosis-based to functional capacity assessments, potentially affecting up to 160,000 participants by 2028. New support programs, Thriving Kids and Foundational Supports, will be introduced for those removed from the NDIS, but details remain unclear. The government will also implement measures to combat fraud within the NDIS, including mandatory registration for more providers and a digital payments system.

title: Clout to over-65s ‘a blow to budget’

This change is expected to negatively impact older Australians, particularly those on pensions, as it may lead to increased public healthcare costs. The health minister argues that the current rebate system is inequitable, but the financial implications of this decision raise concerns about its long-term effects on government budgets.

summary: The SMSF Association has refuted claims that self-managed superannuation funds (SMSFs) are poorly governed, asserting that the sector operates under strict regulatory oversight from the ATO and ASIC. He cautioned against conflating misconduct in investment practices with the SMSF structure itself.

Estate planning will need to adapt to these changes to avoid legal conflicts and ensure fair distribution of assets.

title: Digital driver’s licences coming to WA
summary: Western Australia will introduce digital driver’s licences and a state digital identity by late 2027, supported by a $28.2 million budget allocation. The digital IDs will be available through the Service WA app and major digital wallets, but will remain optional alongside physical licences. The initiative aims to enhance security by limiting the amount of personal information shared when scanned at licensed venues.
